speaker: interviewer
text: --left off Mr. Glucksman, you were at the kitchen window that Sunday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we found out that they're giving us a special dinner, which I still can remember. That was the first time that they gave us a dinner that it was-- it looked more like for human people to eat. The soup had pieces of meat in it. People told us that was horse meant. Everybody was kidding around, which we didn't care. We wished that we had enough horse meat. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then, suddenly, while we staying in the line, a truck comes by towards the kitchen. Dragging behind the truck was a one of the prisoners. You could see that-- we thought first that's a doll or maybe a stuffed uniform with something. Meanwhile, when it came closer, we saw a body ripped apart into pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the body connected to the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guy was trying to escape. They caught him. They tied him back on with a chain by his neck, drove him over raggedy rocks, hills. By the time they brought him back towards the kitchen window, there were like pieces of meat with bones hanging. That's kind of torture that they did to him to kill him. Then they took that body and they put it up on a pole, close by near in the kitchen window. Everybody who went for lunch had to go by it, either touch it with the shoulders or look at it to scare the rest of the prisoners not to do this. Otherwise, the same thing is going to happen to them. That was the most treacherous murder that I ever saw performed on any human being. Even if they would shoot them with 100 bullets that body would've looked better than it did, what I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that ever happen to anyone else while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this at Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason for the better meal that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: For that reason. That was the reason. To show everybody that-- to show off that body and to let them know what's going to happen if anybody else is going to try something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the other prisoners to seeing that.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no reaction. Nobody cared. You just pass by. Either you look up to the body or you look down. You don't care. I mean, you're in a situation where you don't know how long that's going to go on. Everybody almost gave up that you're never going to get out of there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you didn't give up? You, personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I did give up. I did give up. The only thing is, I though I have to wait for my next-- I mean, I just didn't want to run out and try to escape because I knew that if they're going to shoot me, they're going to shoot me at the lake, or on the platz, or that I'm still going to be useful for them. They didn't shoot especially anybody to kill and a lot of the guards, they would say, the bullet is worth more than a Jew. A Jewish life is not worth the price of a bullet. So they were trying to save the bullet for something better than that. They were trying to kill you in another way. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then when we became, after that-- you know, so that you walk to work like an animal. In the winter, when we had to walk to work eight kilometers each way, and we had wooden slippers on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wooden slippers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, they were slippers with a rag, you know, material on top. The soles were made out of wood about an inch thick. In the winter, in the snow, you couldn't walk really with it because the snow sticks to the wood and if you pile up enough snow on it, either you have to chop it off while you're walking or you fall down. And if you-- anybody fell down a little bit out of the line, the German guards would release the dogs that were trained if they see any prisoner a little bit out of the line, to jump at him. And a lot of people got ripped apart pieces just for that reason, when he was trying to chop off the snow from the sole, or if he fell down, he couldn't walk anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So either they had to drag him down to work where we went or you just leave him on the side until a wagon or a truck comes by, collect the body, bring him to prison again. What was your health like while you were at Gross Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was young. I was healthy enough to work, you know? I did the hard labor. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We did loading up those wagons with the earth. We were building autobahns at that time, too, and all day long, with no interruption, we had to load those push carts. I mean, they were huge carts. I don't know what the hell you can call them. They looked like a quarter of a train wagon. And when it was filled up to the top, you had to push it into a ditch and ditch that sand or ground into that ditch, and then bring back the empty wagon and do it again. That's what we had to do all day long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you were building the autobahn-- so when you were going to work, you were walking and leaving the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see people, other people who lived in the nearby towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As you were

speaker: interviewer
text: Walking? What was their reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their reaction? They didn't care. I could tell you about it, but it's something you don't-- about Dachau. To go into Dachau, you're coming in from the main street, Dachauer Strasse. And there are people living on both sides of the street. Every day they could see-- the children could see with their own eyes, bringing in prisoners, loaded up on trucks, or any other vehicle, high up bodies. And the crematorium there was right-- actually maybe like 30 feet from their houses because the crematorium in Dachau was at the main gate, the main entrance, on the left hand side. I can recall it just like-- this I can picture now. And the town must've stunk from the smell from the chimneys because the crematorium was burning there day and night. They had bodies laying outside by the piles of them to be burned. They couldn't catch up ever with the bodies. And when I was liberated, the first thing I thought to myself at that time would be, if I'm going to be able to, I'm going to run into those people to see what they had to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first question was, did you know what went inside across the street? They made believe that they  didn't know a thing about it. They made believe that they didn't know there was a camp in there. But they saw prisoners marching in and out every single day by the thousands, and they denied this. Two days later, after I was liberated, I got into Jerome's-- the soldier that I became friendly with-- I went into him, and I-- you know, I just ask him, I says, don't you-- don't you have a carabine? Because he was carrying a [? sight, ?] you know, a gun. And he says, yes, it's staying over there. You know, they had those short rifles, the outfit. And I took that rifle, and I went in to see the German again. That lived outside the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I says to him, you knew what had went on in there. He started shaking, denying again, until I pointed the rifle at him. Then he says, yes, he knew about it. He was in the army himself but he couldn't do or think about it, and that was the answer of most of them. Most of them, they just like to see the killings of Jews. You know, and I was so full of hate that I pulled the bullet-- that I pulled that-- the, the thing, and I killed him. And nobody of the Eighth Army would say a thing. They didn't care. They gave us almost a week long to go out and do whatever we want. And if we asked any soldier of giving us gun or rifle, they would take off the rifle from their own shoulder and give it to us. Because when they came into camp and they saw with their own eyes what they did see, they sat down and they were crying like kids. That's how terrible it was that they couldn't take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you alone when you went back to the German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was. The main thing that I did go out was, at that day, to go out to look for food. That was the main thing. But when we-- when I saw a man in there, I knew that man had to be-- or had to know. Either he was in the army or he is in the army or he knew everything that had went on in there, especially when you live so close by to the camp. Couldn't have missed it. At the same time, it happened even when the Americans-- the day that we woke up, the last day of the liberation, we went out-- they call it appellplatz, where we gather. You know, all the prisoners get together from the camp to be picked up to go to work, be counted. And it's already daylight. And we don't see anybody of the storm troopers picking us up like normally.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we still wait. Nobody moves. Everybody's afraid. Then all of a sudden you hear heavy, heavy cannon shots. We didn't know where it was coming from. The sky, instead of blue, became like black clouds all over the camp. We looked around. We still see, in the guard houses, high up, moving people. The guards are still there and the machine guns pointing at us. There were like white rags sticking in the-- in the gun. So nobody still don't know what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: We still wait. A few hours later-- like maybe a couple-- two hours-- we hear louder noises in the streets, like heavy trucks or heavy tanks or something heavy and wait. Suddenly, it becomes louder and louder and tanks, one after another, ripping through that gate. They didn't open up the gate. They just went right over it. We looked towards the tanks and we see it's a different color tank. It's not a color of the German tank. The German tanks are dark gray, and this was light green, like the Polish army had. And they were-- we didn't know if they were Russians or Americans. We couldn't tell the difference. We never saw a Russian or American before in our life. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The tanks start coming by the dozens, around and around the camp. Then they stopped. Prisoners that we were waiting to go out to work, we started running towards the-- towards the tanks and the soldiers came over and grabbed us around, hugged us, kissed us. They started to cry like children. They took out from their pockets cigarettes, packs of chocolate, whatever they had to give us. And then when we start making louder noises-- whistling, dancing, running around all over like a celebration-- then a lot of our prisoners-- the guards must've been afraid to come down from over there. But they must have known the night before already that something's going to happen that morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of them were dressed up like we were, in the blue stripe uniforms. And what we had was our hair on our heads was shaved in a cross-- two inches going this way and two inches going this way. So in case you wanted to escape, somebody's going to recognize you. Because the hats that we had to wear that were like berets, you couldn't cover it up. You understand what I mean? You still would see those four clumps of hair hanging down at you. We didn't get hair cuts, except this. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when we saw those guys in those boots, everybody who was able to walk a little bit or to run a little bit-- I remember just like now-- it was right near the main entrance on the left hand side. I ripped off a machine gun from one of the soldiers. I mean, you didn't have to rip them off. If you just went to it that you didn't know how to ask him for it but they just took it off and they gave it to you. I climbed up at that booth and I must've put-- I emptied the magazine-- I don't know how many bullets were in it-- into one storm trooper. His belly opened up like a chicken and the pieces fell down into the water. In Dachau, you had the camp was surrounded with a deep concrete wall, half of it filled up with water after the electric barbed wires, in case somebody wanted to try to escape. First you couldn't because the wire was electrified, so you would get burned right there to death. And if you do go through, you have to go into that water. There was no way of anybody to climb up on a straight concrete wall to get out towards another fence. After the fence, that was why the German was up there in those guard houses. And I remember pieces of that SS falling into that water. The water turned red like blood. That was my biggest pleasure that I had for my parents, for my brothers and sisters, and for everybody, that I was able to pay back a little bit for the tortures that they had to go through.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you climbed up that tower, did you say anything to him? Or him to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Not a word. It made me so mad. Like I was like  a wild dog to see just a man who killed yesterday to wear one of the uniforms today to try to camouflage himself so he could escape that I didn't have any-- I just couldn't ask or say anything. I mean everybody-- it was not just me-- but whoever was able to run around, was strong enough to walk around, everybody was trying to take advantage of that because most of them were still sick. There was typhoid. I was over it. I survived it with no doctor's care, no medicine. People died by the thousands every single day. Dachau had 33,000 to 34,000 prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. And if a thousand died one day, a thousand would be replaced the next day. They had a quota to keep so many prisoners in that camp. And then I heard, after the liberation, while I was still in Dachau, that Dachau was all dynamited the day before. They had to wait for a order to push one button to blow up the whole camp with all the prisoners in it because their command was that there should be no prisoner left alive in that camp. If the Germans saw that they have no-- by the end of the war, to push the button to blow up the whole camp that there shouldn't be a sign of all that went on in there. One of the soldiers-- towards the end, they had a lot of older soldiers in the army-- some of them even 65 years old or so. They would put a uniform on them and they would give them a rifle from the First World War because they were already short on rifles, and he would be a guard-like, you know, because all the younger ones, they had to send to the front lines. But the older ones, they would guard us. So one of those guys, he found out that the camp is dynamited. He went towards the front line on a bicycle to warn the Allied troops. If they want to find Dachau in tact with all the prisoners, they should change their route to get as fast as possible towards Dachau. Otherwise, the next few hours, there's nobody going to be left in the camp. And that was that surprise that morning where the American army came in unexpectedly. That was the end of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Glucksman, I wanted to go back to something you'd mentioned before. After the liberation, when you took the rifle and went to the person's home right outside of the camp, was he alone in his house when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. His wife was there, and I saw one daughter. The daughter must have been already somebody, too, that she was in the army or something because I could tell, at that time, the way she was dressed. She had some army clothes on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's-- I have a few more questions about this but we need to change the tape, so let's just stop quickly for a moment.
